An institutional enquiry could overturn this negative.

access.accessMethod: First ask whether CUL can supply an existing complete digital surrogate or complete microfilm. If none is available, request a full-color reproduction of the whole physical codex: front and back covers, boards and inner boards, spine, pastedowns, every endleaf and flyleaf including the cataloged blank leaf at each end, all stubs, every recto and verso of numbered fols. 1–190, edges if offered, and any other bound surfaces. Identify the target work separately as fols. 1–190, beginning fol. 1 and ending on the final numbered leaf, exact terminal side to be confirmed. Use a target-only copy only if CUL says whole-object capture is infeasible.
access.contact: CUL Imaging Services via the online order form
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26
access.format: Provider-quoted electronic delivery; current guide describes high-resolution uncompressed JPEG, TIFF on request, and high-resolution JPEG for microfilm scans
notes: No enquiry or order has been submitted; availability, condition approval, existing-surrogate coverage, price, delivery format, and reuse terms remain unconfirmed.
Request private-study supply terms and any separate publication or redistribution permission independently.

rights.basis: No images or quote have been supplied and no item-specific image license has been offered. Current order guidance requires copyright declarations, payment, and acceptance of terms.
rights.component: Prospective Cambridge manuscript photographs, delivery files, and reproduction/publication permissions

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: This is the modern catalog's manuscript-production date, distinct from the conventional c. 1449 composition date for the work.

dateStatements.label: later exhibition note
dateStatements.notes: The later note records exhibition before a lord in his chapel at Lambeth; its historical interpretation and the exact legal setting remain debated.

entities.description: The sole extant Repressor manuscript according to Binski and Zutshi's Cambridge catalog. The 1858 catalog describes a small vellum folio of 190 numbered leaves, excluding one blank leaf at each end, written in double columns of about 37 lines in a fifteenth-century hand. The target begins on fol. 1 and ends on the final numbered leaf; the exact terminal side is not stated in the checked catalog evidence.

holding.notes: Current CUL guidance routes many medieval two-letter shelfmarks through the nineteenth-century printed catalog rather than an exact modern online item record.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26
notes: The later title attributes the work to Bishop Pecock, while the author's name does not occur in the body according to the 1858 catalog.
Babington's direct-editorial statement, Pecock's self-references in other works, and later scholarship jointly support authorship; no claim of an autograph main hand is made.
The 1858 catalog's Archbishop Rotheram donation statement is preserved as an older claim; Babington later judged it probably erroneous.

physicalDescription.extent: 190 numbered leaves; the 1858 catalog additionally excludes one blank leaf at each end
physicalDescription.format: small folio; double columns; about 37 lines per column
notes: Initials are illuminated and capitals variously colored; several later hands occur.

The 1858 description therefore accounts for at least 192 leaves, but a current cover-to-cover physical inventory, binding description, stub count, and exact number of additional endleaves were not obtained.

Babington reports at least three early correction hands; Smith reports seeing two and describes their changes as non-substantive. Binski and Zutshi say the manuscript was probably corrected by the author. These remain attributed, non-collapsed claims.

next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME maintainers.
Until then, retain unknown repository-artifact status and do not transfer the software-worded root license to upstream textual, edition, or image layers.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover the inherited U-M XML data, transcription, encoding, and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ahb1325.xml?

next_steps: Ask U-M for the complete image inventory or bundle and production record.
Ask whether the current No Copyright–United States statement applies separately to OCR/XML and TIFF delivery despite the generic 2006 restriction warning.
Compare any supplied images with both independent Internet Archive carriers before asserting copy identity or derivation.
openQuestions.question: Can U-M provide a stable complete download of the 822-image two-volume sequence, identify the scanned physical copies, and clarify exact OCR/XML versus image-layer rights?

next_steps: Use the imaging form to ask first for an existing complete digital surrogate or complete microfilm.

If none exists, request covers, boards and inner boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves/flyleaves and blanks, stubs, every recto and verso of numbered fols. 1–190, edges if offered, and any other bound surfaces; identify fols. 1–190 separately as the work range.
Ask for the complete delivered image inventory and an explicit list of surfaces omitted for condition or handling reasons.
Ask separately for private-study supply, repository/Git redistribution, publication, and commercial/POD terms.
notes: No enquiry or order has yet been submitted; this is a planned mediated route, not pending external correspondence.
openQuestions.question: Can Cambridge University Library supply an existing complete surrogate or, if none exists, a new full-color whole-physical-codex reproduction of Kk.4.26 with an auditable surface inventory and explicit reuse terms?

next_steps: Request a current codicological description or staff-supplied surface inventory together with any reproduction.
Do not convert the 1858 statement '190 leaves excluding a blank leaf at each end' into a claim that the complete current object has exactly 192 leaves or no other surfaces.
Confirm the exact terminal side before adding a fol. 190r/190v locator.
openQuestions.question: What is the current complete physical extent of Kk.4.26, including binding, covers, pastedowns, all endleaves/flyleaves, blanks, stubs, foliation anomalies, and the exact recto/verso endpoint of the Repressor?

next_steps: Compare the whole-codex images paleographically with authenticated or securely attributed Pecock material if available.

Reconcile Babington's at-least-three-hand account, Binski and Zutshi's 'probably corrected by the author,' and Smith's two-hand/non-substantive assessment without treating any one as certain by default.
openQuestions.question: Which correction hands in Kk.4.26 are contemporary, whether any is Pecock's, and whether the manuscript was an authorially supervised or trial-related copy?

next_steps: Check the earliest CUL shelf lists, donor records, ownership inscriptions, and the full provenance discussion in current cataloging.
Retain the 1858 Rotheram claim and Babington's later rejection as separately attributed statements until primary acquisition evidence resolves them.
openQuestions.question: Did Archbishop Thomas Rotheram actually present Kk.4.26 to Cambridge University Library, or is the 1858 catalog attribution a later donor-book inference?

reviewNotes: The primary historical path is repository artifact → U-M uncorrected OCR → U-M 822-image aggregate → Babington 1860 two-volume edition → CUL MS Kk.4.26. The two Internet Archive PDFs are independent volume-specific facsimile routes, not upstream OCR sources unless future production evidence proves that direction.

The target is one medieval prose work, nodes :3–:8 and printed pp. 1–565. Babington's appended Bury excerpts, Abbreviatio, Gascoigne extract, glossary, and index are outside the work boundary even though the U-M image carrier continues through them.

Volume I and volume II are separately modeled bibliographic entities, and each Internet Archive scan is a distinct carrier with its own contributor, production date, access record, local copies, and rights assessment.

The U-M sequence's 822 PB occurrences are 822 unique TIFF references. Target prose pages are sequences 111–675; sequence 110 is a blank inside the prologue container. Both volume title pages were moved into the front of the merged digital sequence.
All 48 XML L elements are Babington apparatus, not Pecock verse: 44 Latin lines in Part II notes and four Middle English lines in a Part III note. The work remains prose-only.

Kk.4.26 contains the target from fol. 1 through the final numbered leaf among 190 numbered leaves. The exact final side, present binding extent, and every non-numbered surface remain open rather than being inferred.

Babington's possible authorial-correction and provenance conclusions, Binski and Zutshi's probable-authorial-correction claim, and Smith's two-corrector/non-substantive assessment remain separately attributed.
Every cached source is recorded once under the entity it delivers. The cache is private/ignored; local presence does not authorize public redistribution.

Kelly's article and Smith's dissertation are provisionally typed catalog_description because schema 1.0.0 has no study entity type; their repository PDFs are separate facsimile/delivery entities and do not enter the textual derivation chain.

No Cambridge manuscript image request was submitted. The manifest records a whole-host-first request specification and a dated no-public-source finding, not pending correspondence, approval, price, delivery, or permission.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: First ask whether CUL can supply an existing complete digital surrogate or complete microfilm. If none is available, request a full-color reproduction of the whole physical codex: front and back covers, boards and inner boards, spine, pastedowns, every endleaf and flyleaf including the cataloged blank leaf at each end, all stubs, every recto and verso of numbered fols. 1–190, edges if offered, and any other bound surfaces. Identify the target work separately as fols. 1–190, beginning fol. 1 and ending on the final numbered leaf, exact terminal side to be confirmed. Use a target-only copy only if CUL says whole-object capture is infeasible.

lineage.summary: The checked-in ahb1325 XML is an exact repository artifact at pinned CME commit e1d77c8, representing University of Michigan item AHB1325.0001.001. U-M describes that 2006 digital item as uncorrected OCR made from and linked to 822 distinct 600 dpi TIFF page images of Churchill Babington's two-volume 1860 first edition. The U-M image sequence aggregates both volumes and places their title pages together before one merged stream; the encoded target is Pecock's prologue and five parts, nodes :3–:8 and printed pp. 1–565, while front matter and the edition's separate back-matter works remain omitted-page milestones. Babington states that he printed the Repressor directly from Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26, whose 190 numbered vellum leaves carry the work from fol. 1 through the final numbered leaf; the 1858 catalog also excludes one blank leaf at each end. Modern Cambridge cataloging calls Kk.4.26 the only extant manuscript and dates its production c. 1449–57, while the work itself is conventionally dated c. 1449. No complete public manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-16, so the practical next route is a whole-codex-first Cambridge imaging enquiry rather than treating the two complete edition scans as manuscript facsimiles.

catalogSummary.author: Reginald Pecock
catalogSummary.date: Composed circa 1449; Kk.4.26, called the only extant manuscript by Binski and Zutshi, dated c. 1449–57; first printed in 1860
catalogSummary.region: England; probably begun at Whittington College in London, but the exact composition history remains qualified
catalogSummary.title: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy

catalogingSubject.label: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Reginald Pecock's single five-part Middle English prose work, including its prologue, as printed on pp. 1–565 of Churchill Babington's two-volume 1860 edition and encoded in U-M/CME nodes AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8. The subject excludes the edition front matter in nodes :1–:2 and the back matter in node :9: excerpts from John Bury's Gladius Salomonis, the Abbreviatio Reginaldi Pecock, an extract from Thomas Gascoigne's theological dictionary, the glossary, and the index. It also excludes Babington's editorial notes as independent work content, the two print volumes and their scans, the U-M OCR and page images, Cambridge University Library MS Kk.4.26 as a physical witness, and the checked-in repository XML as distinct transmission layers.

contentParts.label: The Repressor of Over Much Blaming of the Clergy

locators.value: AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8; sequence 110 is a preceding blank inside :3 and target text is at sequences 111–675
begins fol. 1 and ends on the final numbered leaf among fols. 1–190; exact terminal side unresolved

notes: The root is the only count-bearing content part; the prologue, five part containers, and 83 chapters describe internal structure rather than additional works.
contentParts.summary: The single cataloged intellectual work: Pecock's Middle English prose Repressor, comprising a prologue and five structurally nested parts with 83 chapters.

contentParts.label: Prologue

locators.value: AHB1325.0001.001:3; U-M sequences 110–114, with 110 a preceding blank and text on 111–114

contentParts.summary: Adapts 2 Timothy 4:2 as an instruction that blame requires patience and doctrine, condemns ungrounded attacks on the clergy, identifies eleven disputed practices, names the book, and announces its five-part plan.

contentParts.label: Part I
contentParts.summary: Twenty chapters establish the general argumentative framework: moral law and reason are not reducible to Scripture, scriptural meaning requires learned interpretation, and neither humble reading nor zeal makes a critic infallible. Pecock uses this framework to answer broad accusations against clerical governance.

contentParts.label: Part II
notes: The 44 L elements in this part are Latin lines inside Babington's NOTE1 apparatus, not verse authored by Pecock.
contentParts.summary: Twenty chapters turn to disputed devotional practices, principally the use of images, pilgrimage, relics, and accusations of idolatry, while also treating hymns, ceremonies, and related liturgical evidence.

contentParts.label: Part III

locators.value: AHB1325.0001.001:6; U-M sequences 385–525; chapter 4 ends at sequence 412 / p. 302 and chapter 5 begins at sequence 413 / p. 303

notes: The four L elements on printed p. 323 are a Middle English verse quotation inside Babington's NOTE1 apparatus.
contentParts.summary: Nineteen chapters defend clerical possession of landed property and revenues through scriptural, patristic, and practical arguments, and address simony, avarice, the Donation of Constantine, ecclesiastical courts, and proposals for confiscation.

contentParts.label: Part IV
contentParts.summary: Nine chapters defend differentiated ecclesiastical hierarchy, including papal and episcopal authority, and the church's authority to make laws and canons.

contentParts.label: Part V
contentParts.summary: Fifteen chapters defend the existence and governance of religious orders, then refer objections concerning several remaining practices—saintly and priestly intercession, costly church ornament, sacraments, oaths, warfare, and capital punishment—to Pecock's other books before ending with the work's explicit.

assertion.notes: The display value preserves the sources' approximate c. 1449 dating without converting it into an exact normalized interval.

notes: This work date is separate from Binski and Zutshi's c. 1449–57 date for production of the surviving manuscript.

dateStatements.display: First printed in Churchill Babington's two-volume edition in 1860

extent.description: One intellectual prose work, counted once. Its 89 structural divisions are the prologue container, five part containers, and 83 nested chapter divisions: 20 + 20 + 19 + 9 + 15. Across nodes :3–:8 the XML contains 6 DIV1, 83 DIV2, 89 HEAD, 770 P, 1,043 NOTE1, 21 Q1, 566 PB, no LG, and 48 L. All 48 L are nested inside 16 Q1 in eight Babington NOTE1 apparatus entries: 44 Latin hymn or liturgical lines on printed pp. 200–205 and four Middle English verse lines on p. 323. None is intrinsic verse in Pecock's prose work. The intellectual text occupies printed pp. 1–565; U-M sequence 110 is a preceding blank structurally placed in the prologue node, while sequences 111–675 carry those 565 printed text pages.

formStatements.description: A five-part prose treatise with a prose prologue. All 48 XML L elements belong to Babington's editorial notes rather than Pecock's work text.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic code eng is normalized to enm for Pecock's fifteenth-century text.
Latin hymn lines and four Middle English verse lines tagged L in the XML occur only in Babington's editorial NOTE1 apparatus and therefore do not create a second work language or a verse component.
The work boundary follows Pecock's prologue and explicit, not Babington's whole two-volume publication or U-M's whole 822-image sequence.
The source header's 'Recock, Reginald' is preserved as an error/variant while the responsible author is normalized as Reginald Pecock.

The Repressor is one intellectual prose work and is counted once. Its 89 structural divisions are one prologue container, five part containers, and 83 nested chapter divisions: Part I 20, Part II 20, Part III 19, Part IV 9, and Part V 15.
Raw L counts do not determine form: all 48 L occur in Babington's notes and intrinsic work verse_lines remains zero.
The only-extant-manuscript claim is attributed to Binski and Zutshi's Cambridge catalog; it is not broadened into a claim that no fragmentary or lost evidence can ever emerge.

next_steps: Review the textual chronology and documentary arguments in current Pecock scholarship before converting the qualified London beginning into a full place-of-composition claim.
Keep the c. 1449 work date separate from the c. 1449–57 production date assigned to Kk.4.26.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact chronology and location of composition beyond the conventional c. 1449 date and Kelly's statement that the work was begun while Pecock was at Whittington College?

assertion.notes: Kelly says the Repressor was begun while Pecock was still at Whittington College; that supports a qualified London beginning, not a complete composition itinerary.

scope.description: The complete Repressor text on printed pp. 1–565, transcribed from Kk.4.26 with the editorial interventions described in Babington's introduction

subjects.label: Reason, Scripture, interpretation, and church authority
Images, pilgrimage, relics, liturgy, and accusations of idolatry
Clerical property, landed endowment, revenues, and confiscation
Papacy, episcopacy, church hierarchy, and ecclesiastical law
Religious orders and other contested ecclesiastical practices

summaries.value: Pecock answers lay and Lollard criticism of eleven clerical practices by insisting that blame must be grounded in patient, demonstrable doctrine. The first part develops his general case for reasoned moral judgment and against treating private scriptural interpretation as self-validating. The remaining parts defend images and pilgrimage; relics and liturgical practices; clerical landholding and endowment; papal and episcopal hierarchy and church law; and religious orders, while referring several remaining controversies to his other writings. The work does not excuse genuine clerical wrongdoing: its polemical target is criticism Pecock regards as unproved or misdirected.

The cataloged work is the prologue and five parts, XML nodes AHB1325.0001.001:3–:8 and Babington pp. 1–565. The source XML places both 1860 volume title pages together before a merged page sequence. Volume I carries the work through Part III, chapter 4, p. 302; Volume II resumes with Part III, chapter 5, p. 303. Node :9 begins the excluded multi-work back matter.

notes: Pecock gives this self-name near the end of the prologue.
